original_abstract:
What defines the individuals responsible for the most profound breakthroughs in human knowledge? This study examines the demographic, institutional, and economic profiles of history’s preeminent scientists—encompassing all Nobel Prize laureates and major non-Nobel discoverers—to identify the drivers behind over 750 of science’s greatest advances. Our analysis reveals that interdisciplinary expertise is a primary catalyst for innovation: scientists with degrees in two or more distinct fields accounted for 54% of Nobel-winning discoveries and 42% of other major breakthroughs, suggesting that diverse methodological training is essential for pioneering work. 

The data also point toward an increasing concentration of scientific influence within elite institutions, with the top 25 universities producing 30% of all landmark discoveries. Age remains a critical factor, as productivity peaks early: researchers over the age of 50 contributed only 7% of Nobel-winning work, while those over 60 accounted for a mere 1%. Furthermore, the "recognition gap"—the time elapsed between a discovery and the receipt of a Nobel Prize—is widening across all disciplines, indicating a lengthening verification and selection process for major milestones.

Beyond these trends, we evaluate the impact of geographic location, gender, religious affiliation, and national economic conditions on scientific output. Our findings suggest that the current trajectory toward narrow specialization may be hindering progress. To maximize future discovery, funding agencies and research institutions should pivot away from traditional silos and instead incentivize interdisciplinary frameworks that integrate novel methodologies from disparate fields.
humanized_abstract:
Who are the people that have brought about the most significant discoveries in humankind’s understanding of the world? By examining the characteristics of the individuals, organisations and societies of today’s leading scientists — all the Nobel Prize winners and major non-Nobel discoverers of every significant piece of knowledge that has been amassed, with the ability to assess their work retrospectively and objectively — researchers have identified what has brought about over 750 of the most significant scientific achievements in history. Their finding is that nearly all of the great advances of science have been made by people with knowledge of two different fields of science. Nobel prize winning discoveries were made by scientists with 54 per cent dual qualifications and 42 per cent in other significant advances. The researchers say that an understanding of two areas of science is needed for any major breakthrough to occur.

The data show that research is becoming more concentrated in the elite universities and that the young are the ones that do the work — with researchers over 50 years of age only contributing 7% of the Nobel Prizes and over 60 only 1%. They also show that the recognition gap between discoveries and Nobel Prizes is increasing in all fields, which means that the time it takes for the scientific community to verify and acknowledge the most important discoveries is growing. This implies that there is a growing selection period between the discovery and the recognition of a scientific breakthrough.

The study explores trends in the global distribution of publications at various levels as well as the effects of location, gender, religion and national economy on scientific productivity. Concluding, the study identifies factors influencing the current trend towards a highly specialized science and presents suggestions and recommendations for further improvements. The findings on the current trend of the science towards highly specialized and reductionist knowledge production emphasize the need for changes to current funding practices and structures in order to promote and support truly interdisciplinary research opportunities that can benefit from advances in other fields of research.

What defines the individuals responsible for the most profound breakthroughs in human knowledge?
